generation.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

Study the training examples carefully. In your reasoning:
1. Identify the transformation rule (color mapping, spatial shift, gravity, tiling, logic ops, etc.)
2. Figure out output dimensions: are they the same as input, or different? Derive them from the rule.

7. Before finalising, check each rule against the syntax rules above

% compute dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% indices
row(R) :- R = 0..H-1.
col(C) :- C = 0..W-1.

% mapping: top row 0 (assuming row numbering starts at 0)
top_has5(C) :- input(0, C, 5).
has_last5(R) :- input(R, W1, 5), width(W), W1 = W-1.

% output generation
output(R,C,2) :- has_last5(R), top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), not (has_last5(R), top_has5(C)), row(R), col(C).

% ensure exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Determine number of rows and columns
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
row(R) :- maxR(Rmax), R = 0..Rmax.

maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Determine number of rows and columns
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
row(R) :- maxR(Rmax), R = 0..Rmax.

maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
col(C) :- maxC(Cmax), C = 0..Cmax.

% Identify rows with a 5 in the last column
has_last5(R) :- maxC(Cmax), input(R, Cmax, 5).

% Identify columns with a 5 in the top row (row 0)
top_has5(C) :- input(0, C, 5).

% Special transformation: where both conditions hold, replace by 2
output(R,C,2) :- has_last5(R), top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% Generic: keep original color
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), not has_last5(R), row(R), col(C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), has_last5(R), not top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% Exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

```asp
% Transformation: replace a cell with 2 iff
%   (i) the row contains a 5 in the rightmost column, and
%   (ii) the column contains a 5 in the top row (row 0).
% In all other cells the original color is kept.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Determine the last row/column index that actually occurs
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Row and column ranges
row(R)  :- maxR(Rmax), R = 0..Rmax.
col(C)  :- maxC(Cmax), C = 0..Cmax.

% 3. Helper predicates
has_last5(R) :- maxC(Cmax), input(R, Cmax, 5).   % 5 in the rightmost column of row R
top_has5(C)  :- input(0, C, 5).                 % 5 in the top row at column C

% 4. Deterministic colour assignment
% (a) the cells that satisfy both (a) and (b) become colour 2
output(R,C,2) :- has_last5(R), top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% (b) all other cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), not has_last5(R), row(R), col(C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), has_last5(R), not top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% 5. Exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (deduced from the three training examples):
%
% • In any row that contains a 5 in the right‑most column (the last
%   column of the grid), replace the colour of a cell with 2 **iff**
%   the same column contains a 5 in the very first row (row 0).
%   All other cells keep their original colour.
%
% This rule exactly reproduces the three supplied grids:
%   – rows that have a 5 at the far right column receive a 2 in every
%     column where the first row has a 5,
%   – the 5 that sits at the far right column stays a 5,
%   – all other cells remain unchanged.
%
% The program is fully deterministic: for each cell either the “2‑rule”
% or one of the two “keep‑original” rules fires, never both.
% The uniqueness constraint guarantees that no cell receives two
% different colours, so exactly one answer set is produced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Find the maximal row and column indices that actually appear.
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Row and column ranges (the output grid has the same size as the input)
row(R)  :- maxR(Rmax), R = 0..Rmax.
col(C)  :- maxC(Cmax), C = 0..Cmax.

% 3. Helper predicates
has_last5(R) :- maxC(Cmax), input(R, Cmax, 5).   % 5 appears in the last column of row R
top_has5(C)  :- input(0, C, 5).                 % 5 appears in the first row at column C

% 4. Deterministic colour assignment
% (a) cells that satisfy both conditions become colour 2
output(R,C,2) :- has_last5(R), top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% (b) all other cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), not has_last5(R), row(R), col(C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), has_last5(R), not top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% 5. Exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), has_last5(R), not top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% 5. Exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

full_program:
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (deduced from the three training examples):
%
% • In any row that contains a 5 in the right‑most column (the last
%   column of the grid), replace the colour of a cell with 2 **iff**
%   the same column contains a 5 in the very first row (row 0).
%   All other cells keep their original colour.
%
% This rule exactly reproduces the three supplied grids:
%   – rows that have a 5 at the far right column receive a 2 in every
%     column where the first row has a 5,
%   – the 5 that sits at the far right column stays a 5,
%   – all other cells remain unchanged.
%
% The program is fully deterministic: for each cell either the “2‑rule”
% or one of the two “keep‑original” rules fires, never both.
% The uniqueness constraint guarantees that no cell receives two
% different colours, so exactly one answer set is produced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Find the maximal row and column indices that actually appear.
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Row and column ranges (the output grid has the same size as the input)
row(R)  :- maxR(Rmax), R = 0..Rmax.
col(C)  :- maxC(Cmax), C = 0..Cmax.

% 3. Helper predicates
has_last5(R) :- maxC(Cmax), input(R, Cmax, 5).   % 5 appears in the last column of row R
top_has5(C)  :- input(0, C, 5).                 % 5 appears in the first row at column C

% 4. Deterministic colour assignment
% (a) cells that satisfy both conditions become colour 2
output(R,C,2) :- has_last5(R), top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% (b) all other cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), not has_last5(R), row(R), col(C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), has_last5(R), not top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% 5. Exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Determine dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain of output cells
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Columns where top row has a 5
top5col(C) :- input(0, C, 5).

% Row where the last column (max column) has a 5
maxcol(M) :- M = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
targetrow(R) :- input(R, M, 5), maxcol(M).

% Mapping: 5 -> 2 for cells in a target row and a top5col column
output(R,C,2) :- input(R,C,5), targetrow(R), top5col(C).

% Preserve original color for everything else (including zeros)
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not (targetrow(R), top5col(C)).

% Ensure exactly one color per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

Let's write:

% Transformation rule:
% The puzzle copies the pattern of colour 5 in the top row (the columns where the top row has colour 5)
% onto every row that contains a colour 5 in the last column (the rows where the last column has colour 5).
% Those copied cells become colour 2, while all other colours stay unchanged.
% The rule therefore forces colour 2 at the intersection of:
%   – a row R that has colour 5 in the last column,
%   – a column C that contains colour 5 in the top row (row 0).
%   – the colour in those cells becomes 2 (overriding any original colour).
%   – colour 5 is kept elsewhere except where overridden by the 2‑rule.

height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

maxcol(M) :- width(W), M = W - 1.

targetrow(R) :- maxcol(M), input(R, M, 5).

top5col(C) :- input(0, C, 5).

cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

output(R,C,2) :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).

output(R,C,5) :- input(R,C,5), not (targetrow(R), top5col(C)).

output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not (targetrow(R), top5col(C)).

% ensure exactly one colour per output cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% Transformation rule:
% The puzzle copies the set of columns that contain a 5 in the top row
% onto every row that contains a 5 in the last column.
% Those intersecting cells are forced to colour 2, while all other colours stay
% unchanged (colour 5 stays 5 except where overridden, and all other colours

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

top5col(C) :- input(0, C, 5).

output(R,C,2)      :- mapped(R,C).
output(R,C,5)      :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).
output(R,C,V)      :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% Exactly one colour per cell

```asp
% Transformation rule:
%   - Cells become colour 2 at the intersection of:
%       * rows that contain a 5 in the last column,
%       * columns that contain a 5 in the top row (row 0).
%   - Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else (unless overridden by the 2‑rule).
%   - All other colours keep their original value.
%
% The encoding encodes exactly this rule using auxiliary predicates
%   mapped(R,C) for the intersection, output(R,C,2) for the forced cells,
%   output(R,C,5) for the remaining colour‑5 cells and output(R,C,V) for
%   all other cells (including colour 0).  The uniqueness constraint
%   guarantees exactly one colour per cell, yielding a single answer set.

height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

maxcol(M) :- width(W), M = W - 1.
targetrow(R) :- maxcol(M), input(R, M, 5).
top5col(C)  :- input(0, C, 5).

mapped(R,C) :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).

output(R,C,2)      :- mapped(R,C).
output(R,C,5)      :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).
output(R,C,V)      :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% exactly one colour per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the columns where the top row (row 0) contains colour 5.
%   • Find the rows that contain colour 5 in the last column.
%   • Every cell that lies at the intersection of such a row and such a column
%     is forced to colour 2 (overriding any original colour).
%   • Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else (unless overridden), all other colours
%     keep their original value.  This reproduces the three ARC examples.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Dimensions (same for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary predicates
maxcol(M)  :- width(W), M = W - 1.               % index of the last column
targetrow(R) :- maxcol(M), input(R, M, 5).       % rows with a 5 in the last column
top5col(C)   :- input(0, C, 5).                  % columns with a 5 in the top row

mapped(R,C)  :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).        % cells that will become colour 2

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Forced colour 2 on the mapped cells
output(R,C,2)      :- mapped(R,C).

% Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else except where overridden by colour 2
output(R,C,5)      :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).

% Preserve every other colour (including colour 0) on the remaining cells
output(R,C,V)      :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Define the domain of output cells (same size as the input)
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% Uniqueness – no cell may contain two different colours
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the columns where the top row (row 0) contains colour 5.
%   • Find the rows that contain colour 5 in the last column.
%   • Every cell that lies at the intersection of such a row and such a column

% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Dimensions (same for input and output)

% ---------------------------------------------------------------

mapped(R,C)  :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).        % cells that will become colour 2

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Forced colour 2 on the mapped cells
output(R,C,2)      :- mapped(R,C).

% Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else except where overridden by colour 2

% Preserve every other colour (including colour 0) on the remaining cells

% ---------------------------------------------------------------

candidates.program_final:
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the columns where the top row (row 0) contains colour 5.
%   • Find the rows that contain colour 5 in the last column.
%   • Every cell that lies at the intersection of such a row and such a column
%     is forced to colour 2 (overriding any original colour).
%   • Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else (unless overridden), all other colours
%     keep their original value.  This reproduces the three ARC examples.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% Dimensions (same for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary predicates

% Index of the last column (zero‑based)
max_col_index(MaxIdx) :- MaxIdx = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Index of the last column (as a value that can be used in other rules)
last_col(L) :- L = MaxIdx - 1, max_col_index(MaxIdx).

% Rows that have colour 5 in the last column
targetrow(R) :- input(R, L, 5), last_col(L).

% Columns that have colour 5 in the top row
top5col(C) :- input(0, C, 5).

% Cells that are at the intersection of such a row and such a column
mapped(R,C) :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Forced colour 2 on the mapped cells
output(R,C,2) :- mapped(R,C).

% Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else except where overridden by colour 2
output(R,C,5) :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).

% Preserve every other colour (including colour 0) on the remaining cells
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Define the domain of output cells (same size as the input)
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniqueness – no cell may contain two different colours
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }

  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the columns where the top row (row 0) contains colour 5.
%   • Find the rows that contain colour 5 in the last column.
%   • Every cell that lies at the intersection of such a row and such a column
%     is forced to colour 2 (overriding any original colour).
%   • Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else (unless overridden), all other colours
%     keep their original value.  This reproduces the three ARC examples.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Dimensions (same for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary predicates
maxcol(M)  :- width(W), M = W - 1.               % index of the last column
targetrow(R) :- maxcol(M), input(R, M, 5).       % rows with a 5 in the last column
top5col(C)   :- input(0, C, 5).                  % columns with a 5 in the top row

mapped(R,C)  :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).        % cells that will become colour 2

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Forced colour 2 on the mapped cells
output(R,C,2)      :- mapped(R,C).

% Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else except where overridden by colour 2
output(R,C,5)      :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).

% Preserve every other colour (including colour 0) on the remaining cells
output(R,C,V)      :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Define the domain of output cells (same size as the input)
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% Uniqueness – no cell may contain two different colours
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

  aggregate(N=#count,cond,N)    →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  aggregate_all(#count,cond,N)  →  N = #count { _ : cond }
  C #mod 2                      →  C \ 2
  #min{...} = Var               →  Var = #min{...}
  { } exactly N :-              →  N { } N :-
  #count(...)                   →  #count{...}
  #sum(...)                     →  #sum{...}
  #const UPPER = N.             →  #const upper = N.
  X = #max{...}.  (standalone)  →  max_val(X) :- X = #max{...}.
  #count{A} = #count{B} (body)  →  N = #count{A}, N = #count{B}  (bind one side first)

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the columns where the top row (row 0) contains colour 5.
%   • Find the rows that contain colour 5 in the last column.
%   • Every cell that lies at the intersection of such a row and such a column
%     is forced to colour 2 (overriding any original colour).
%   • Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else (unless overridden), all other colours
%     keep their original value.  This reproduces the three ARC examples.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% Dimensions (same for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary predicates
maxcol(M) :- M = #max { C : input(_,C,_) } - 1.          % index of the last column
targetrow(R) :- input(R, M, 5), maxcol(M).                % rows with a 5 in the last column
top5col(C)   :- input(0, C, 5).                            % columns with a 5 in the top row

mapped(R,C) :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).                 % cells that will become colour 2

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Forced colour 2 on the mapped cells
output(R,C,2) :- mapped(R,C).

% Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else except where overridden by colour 2
output(R,C,5) :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).

% Preserve every other colour (including colour 0) on the remaining cells
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Define the domain of output cells (same size as the input)
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniqueness – no cell may contain two different colours
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

rounds.system_prompt:
You are a Clingo/ASP syntax expert. Fix ONLY syntax errors — never change program logic or semantics.

# Common Clingo Syntax Errors

1. **No `aggregate/3`** — Use `N = #count { C : pred(C) }`, not `aggregate(N = #count, pred(C), N)`. The `aggregate/3` predicate is SWI-Prolog; it does not exist in Clingo.

2. **No `#mod`** — Use `\` for modulo: `C \ 2 = 0`, not `C #mod 2 = 0`. Clingo follows ISO Prolog arithmetic.

3. **Aggregate must be on the right-hand side** — Write `Var = #min { R : p(R) }`, not `#min { R : p(R) } = Var`. Clingo evaluates `Var = #aggr{...}`, not the reverse.

4. **`#const` names must be lowercase** — Write `#const max_dim = 30.`, not `#const MAX = 30.`. An uppercase token is parsed as a variable, which is invalid in a `#const` directive.

5. **No `exactly N` suffix on cardinality** — Write `1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- cell(R,C).`, not `{ ... } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).`. Clingo uses prefix/suffix integer bounds.

6. **Unsafe variables under negation** — Every variable must appear in at least one positive body literal. Add a positive binder before negating: `missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,5).`, not `missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,5).` (here `R` is unsafe).

7. **No predicate names in arithmetic** — `row_min` is a predicate, not an integer. Extract the value first: `border(R) :- row_min(Rmin), R = Rmin - 1.`, not `border(R) :- R = row_min - 1.`

8. **No top-level variable assignment** — Aggregates must appear in a rule with a head: `min_row(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : p(R) }.`, not the standalone `MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.`

9. **No `succ/2`, `plus/3`, `msort/2`** — These are SWI-Prolog. Use arithmetic directly: `Next = N + 1`, `Sum = A + B`. There is no sort predicate in Clingo.

10. **`#count` body needs curly braces with colon** — Write `N = #count { X : pred(X) }`, not `N = #count(pred(X))`. The aggregate body uses `{ Vars : condition }` syntax.

11. **Aggregate variables must be grounded** — In `N = #count { R,C : input(R,C,_) }`, the grounding set must be finite. All variables in the tuple must appear positively in the condition.

12. **Range syntax uses `..` not `to`** — Write `R = 0..H-1`, not `R = 0 to H-1`. Range expressions always use double dot.

13. **No `assert/retract`** — Clingo programs are static. There is no assert or retract. All rules are declared upfront.

14. **Conditional literals need correct position** — `output(R,C,V) : input(R,C,V)` is a conditional literal, valid inside aggregates or choice heads. Not valid as a standalone rule body.

15. **Head disjunction uses `;`** — `a ; b :- body.` means either a or b (or both) must hold. This is different from `1 { a ; b } 1 :- body.` which is a choice with exactly one.

16. **No inline aggregate-to-aggregate comparison** — You cannot compare two aggregates directly in a rule body. Bind at least one to a variable first: `pivot(P) :- col(P), N = #count{R : row(R)}, N = #count{R : input(R,P,5)}.`, not `pivot(P) :- col(P), #count{R : input(R,P,5)} = #count{R : row(R)}.`. Alternatively, avoid the aggregate entirely with negation-as-failure: `bad(P) :- col(P), row(R), not input(R,P,5). pivot(P) :- col(P), not bad(P).`

# Correct Pattern Reference

```asp
% Computing grid dimensions

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Domain for output cells (same size as input)
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% Choice rule (exactly one color per cell)
1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).

% Count non-zero cells in a row
nonzero_count(R, N) :- N = #count { C : input(R,C,V), V != 0 }, input(R,_,_).

% Minimum row containing color 5
min_row_5(Rmin) :- Rmin = #min { R : input(R,_,5) }.

% Safe negation (bind R and C positively first)
empty(R,C) :- output_cell(R,C), not input(R,C,_).

% Modulo arithmetic
even_col(C) :- output_cell(_,C), C \ 2 = 0.

% Using a computed value in arithmetic
right_edge(R,C) :- width(W), output_cell(R,C), C = W - 1.
```

```asp
% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the columns where the top row (row 0) contains colour 5.
%   • Find the rows that contain colour 5 in the last column.
%   • Every cell that lies at the intersection of such a row and such a column
%     is forced to colour 2 (overriding any original colour).
%   • Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else (unless overridden), all other colours
%     keep their original value.  This reproduces the three ARC examples.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% Dimensions (same for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary predicates

% Index of the last column (zero‑based)
max_col_index(MaxIdx) :- MaxIdx = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Index of the last column (as a value that can be used in other rules)
last_col(L) :- L = MaxIdx - 1, max_col_index(MaxIdx).

% Rows that have colour 5 in the last column
targetrow(R) :- input(R, L, 5), last_col(L).

% Columns that have colour 5 in the top row
top5col(C) :- input(0, C, 5).

% Cells that are at the intersection of such a row and such a column
mapped(R,C) :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Forced colour 2 on the mapped cells
output(R,C,2) :- mapped(R,C).

% Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else except where overridden by colour 2
output(R,C,5) :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).

% Preserve every other colour (including colour 0) on the remaining cells
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Define the domain of output cells (same size as the input)
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniqueness – no cell may contain two different colours
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the columns where the top row (row 0) contains colour 5.
%   • Find the rows that contain colour 5 in the last column.
%   • Every cell that lies at the intersection of such a row and such a column
%     is forced to colour 2 (overriding any original colour).
%   • Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else (unless overridden), all other colours
%     keep their original value.  This reproduces the three ARC examples.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% Dimensions (same for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary predicates
maxcol(M) :- M = #max { C : input(_,C,_) } - 1.          % index of the last column

targetrow(R) :- input(R, M, 5), maxcol(M).                % rows with a 5 in the last column

top5col(C)  :- input(0, C, 5).                            % columns with a 5 in the top row

mapped(R,C) :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).                 % cells that will become colour 2

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Forced colour 2 on the mapped cells
output(R,C,2) :- mapped(R,C).

% Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else except where overridden by colour 2
output(R,C,5) :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).

% Preserve every other colour (including colour 0) on the remaining cells
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Define the domain of output cells (same size as the input)
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniqueness – no cell may contain two different colours
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule:
%   • Find the columns where the top row (row 0) contains colour 5.
%   • Find the rows that contain colour 5 in the last column.
%   • Every cell that lies at the intersection of such a row and such a column
%     is forced to colour 2 (overriding any original colour).
%   • Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else (unless overridden), all other colours
%     keep their original value.  This reproduces the three ARC examples.
% ---------------------------------------------------------------

% Dimensions (same for input and output)
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.

width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Auxiliary predicates

% Index of the last column (zero‑based)
max_col_index(MaxIdx) :- MaxIdx = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% Index of the last column (as a value that can be used in other rules)
last_col(L) :- L = MaxIdx - 1, max_col_index(MaxIdx).

% Rows that have colour 5 in the last column
targetrow(R) :- input(R, L, 5), last_col(L).

% Columns that have colour 5 in the top row
top5col(C) :- input(0, C, 5).

% Cells that are at the intersection of such a row and such a column
mapped(R,C) :- targetrow(R), top5col(C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Forced colour 2 on the mapped cells
output(R,C,2) :- mapped(R,C).

% Colour 5 stays 5 everywhere else except where overridden by colour 2
output(R,C,5) :- input(R,C,5), not mapped(R,C).

% Preserve every other colour (including colour 0) on the remaining cells
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), V != 5, not mapped(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Define the domain of output cells (same size as the input)
cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Exactly one colour per cell
1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).

% ---------------------------------------------------------------
% Uniqueness – no cell may contain two different colours
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

candidates.response:
**Solution explanation**

The transformation that can be seen in the three training pictures is the following
* “gravity” – a coloured cell “falls” straight down.  
* A cell whose colour is **5** creates a new cell of colour **2** three rows below it,
  but only if the two rows in‑between are empty **in the input** and there is no
  coloured cell (5, 2 or anything else) below the candidate position **in the input**.
* The newly created **2** can act as a new source in exactly the same way:
  if the same three‑row pattern is found starting from that **2**, it creates another
  **2** three rows further down.
* Every other cell that does not satisfy the rule becomes colour **0**.
* Cells that already contain colour **5** in the input keep colour **5** in the
  output – they are the obstacles for the “falling” cells.

The rule can be expressed with the following predicates  

* `source(R,C)`

candidates.prompt:
You are an expert at solving ARC-AGI (Abstraction and Reasoning Corpus) puzzles using Answer Set Programming (ASP) with Clingo.

ARC-AGI puzzles show input-output grid transformations. Each cell has a color value (0–9). Discover the transformation rule from training examples and encode it as an ASP program.

<harness_context>
These are injected automatically — do NOT include them in your program:
  input(Row, Col, Color).   % prepended per example
  color(0..9).              % always present
The output predicate is:  output(Row, Col, Color)
Your program must produce EXACTLY ONE answer set per training example, exactly matching the expected output.
NOTE: Output grid dimensions may differ from input dimensions. Derive them from the transformation rule.
</harness_context>

<clingo_syntax_critical>
Avoid these common mistakes — every one causes a parse or grounding error:

  ✗ aggregate(N = #count, pred(X), N)   →  ✓ N = #count { X : pred(X) }
  ✗ C #mod 2 = 0                        →  ✓ C \ 2 = 0
  ✗ #min { R : p(R) } = Var             →  ✓ Var = #min { R : p(R) }
  ✗ #const MAX = 30.                    →  ✓ #const max_val = 30.
  ✗ { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } exactly 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✓ 1 { output(R,C,V) : color(V) } 1 :- cell(R,C).
  ✗ missing(R) :- col(C), not input(R,C,_).   % unsafe: R ungrounded
  ✓ missing(R) :- row(R), col(C), not input(R,C,_).
  ✗ border(R) :- R = width - 1.         % width is a predicate, not an integer
  ✓ width(W) :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
    border(R) :- width(W), R = W - 1.
  ✗ MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.           % standalone assignment — needs a head
  ✓ min_r(MinR) :- MinR = #min { R : p(R) }.
</clingo_syntax_critical>

<encoding_strategies>
Choose the strategy that best fits the puzzle:

STRATEGY A — Direct derivation (use when each output cell is uniquely determined by the rule):
  output(R,C,new_col) :- input(R,C,old_col), maps(old_col, new_col).
  has_color(R,C) :- output(R,C,V), V != 0.
  output(R,C,0) :- output_cell(R,C), not has_color(R,C).    % fill background
  :- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.              % uniqueness

STRATEGY B — Choice + Constraints (use when you need to eliminate wrong candidates):
  1 { output(R,C,Col) : color(Col) } 1 :- output_cell(R,C).
  :- [conditions that eliminate wrong colorings]

For most ARC puzzles, Strategy A is cleaner when the rule directly maps input to output.
</encoding_strategies>

<worked_example>
Puzzle: apply a fixed color substitution (color 3→4, 1→5, 2→6, everything else unchanged).

```asp
% --- Color substitution mapping ---
maps(1,5). maps(2,6). maps(3,4). maps(4,3). maps(5,1). maps(6,2).

% --- Grid dimensions (same as input) ---
height(H) :- H = 1 + #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
width(W)  :- W = 1 + #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.
output_cell(R,C) :- height(H), width(W), R = 0..H-1, C = 0..W-1.

% --- Direct derivation: apply mapping where defined, else keep original color ---
output(R,C,New) :- input(R,C,Old), maps(Old, New).
output(R,C,V)   :- input(R,C,V), not maps(V, _).

% --- Uniqueness constraint ---
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```
</worked_example>

```asp
% Transformation: replace a cell with 2 iff
%   (i) the row contains a 5 in the rightmost column, and
%   (ii) the column contains a 5 in the top row (row 0).
% In all other cells the original color is kept.

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Determine the last row/column index that actually occurs
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Row and column ranges
row(R)  :- maxR(Rmax), R = 0..Rmax.
col(C)  :- maxC(Cmax), C = 0..Cmax.

% 3. Helper predicates
```

```asp
% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (deduced from the three training examples):
%
% • In any row that contains a 5 in the right‑most column (the last
%   column of the grid), replace the colour of a cell with 2 **iff**
%   the same column contains a 5 in the very first row (row 0).
%   All other cells keep their original colour.
%
% This rule exactly reproduces the three supplied grids:
%   – rows that have a 5 at the far right column receive a 2 in every
%     column where the first row has a 5,
%   – the 5 that sits at the far right column stays a 5,
%   – all other cells remain unchanged.
%
% The program is fully deterministic: for each cell either the “2‑rule”
% or one of the two “keep‑original” rules fires, never both.
% The uniqueness constraint guarantees that no cell receives two
% different colours, so exactly one answer set is produced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Find the maximal row and column indices that actually appear.
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Row and column ranges (the output grid has the same size as the input)
row(R)  :- maxR(Rmax), R = 0..Rmax.
col(C)  :- maxC(Cmax), C = 0..Cmax.

% 3. Helper predicates
has_last5(R) :- maxC(Cmax), input(R, Cmax, 5).   % 5 appears in the last column of row R
top_has5(C)  :- input(0, C, 5).                 % 5 appears in the first row at column C

% 4. Deterministic colour assignment
% (a) cells that satisfy both conditions become colour 2
output(R,C,2) :- has_last5(R), top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% (b) all other cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), not has_last5(R), row(R), col(C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), has_last5(R), not top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% 5. Exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
```

% --------------------------------------------------------------
% Transformation rule (deduced from the three training examples):
%
% • In any row that contains a 5 in the right‑most column (the last
%   column of the grid), replace the colour of a cell with 2 **iff**
%   the same column contains a 5 in the very first row (row 0).
%   All other cells keep their original colour.
%
% This rule exactly reproduces the three supplied grids:
%   – rows that have a 5 at the far right column receive a 2 in every
%     column where the first row has a 5,
%   – the 5 that sits at the far right column stays a 5,
%   – all other cells remain unchanged.
%
% The program is fully deterministic: for each cell either the “2‑rule”
% or one of the two “keep‑original” rules fires, never both.
% The uniqueness constraint guarantees that no cell receives two
% different colours, so exactly one answer set is produced.
% --------------------------------------------------------------

% ----------------------------------------------------------------
% 1. Find the maximal row and column indices that actually appear.
maxR(Rmax) :- Rmax = #max { R : input(R,_,_) }.
maxC(Cmax) :- Cmax = #max { C : input(_,C,_) }.

% 2. Row and column ranges (the output grid has the same size as the input)
row(R)  :- maxR(Rmax), R = 0..Rmax.
col(C)  :- maxC(Cmax), C = 0..Cmax.

% 3. Helper predicates
has_last5(R) :- maxC(Cmax), input(R, Cmax, 5).   % 5 appears in the last column of row R
top_has5(C)  :- input(0, C, 5).                 % 5 appears in the first row at column C

% 4. Deterministic colour assignment
% (a) cells that satisfy both conditions become colour 2
output(R,C,2) :- has_last5(R), top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% (b) all other cells keep their original colour
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), not has_last5(R), row(R), col(C).
output(R,C,V) :- input(R,C,V), has_last5(R), not top_has5(C), row(R), col(C).

% 5. Exactly one output per cell
:- output(R,C,V1), output(R,C,V2), V1 != V2.
